core_meaning: Formal question marker placing est-ce que at the beginning of statement
primary_communicative_fn: Asking yes/no questions in neutral/formal tone
when_to_use: Convert statements into yes/no questions without verb inversion
key_variations: Est-ce que tu viens à la fête? (Are you coming to the party?).
Est-ce qu'il a fini son travail? (Did he finish his work?).
Est-ce que nous partons demain? (Are we leaving tomorrow?).
Est-ce qu'elles connaissent la réponse? (Do they know the answer?).
essential_vocabulary_slots: être, avoir, venir, finir, partir, connaître, comprendre
common_learner_mistakes: Combining est-ce que with inversion: *« est-ce que viens-tu »
Omitting 'que' before vowels: *« est-ce il » instead of « est-ce qu'il »
Using est-ce que with already-inverted questions
primary_function: Forming yes/no questions with informal inversion
essential_vocabulary: être, avoir, venir, finir, partir, connaître, comprendre, aller
why_it_matters: Est-ce que is the simplest question formation—no verb inversion needed

structure_label: Inversion du sujet (subject-verb inversion in questions)
core_meaning: Direct inversion of subject and verb to form questions in formal French
primary_communicative_fn: Asking formal questions with inverted word order
when_to_use: In formal speech/writing, invert verb and subject with hyphens
key_variations: Viens-tu demain? (Are you coming tomorrow?).
A-t-il terminé? (Has he finished?).
Sortez-vous ce soir? (Are you going out tonight?).
Viendront-ils à temps? (Will they arrive on time?).
essential_vocabulary_slots: venir, terminer, sortir, arriver, faire, avoir, être
common_learner_mistakes: Omitting hyphens: *« viens tu » instead of « viens-tu »
Not inserting -t- with vowel-initial verbs: *« a-il » instead of « a-t-il »
Inverting nouns and verbs (use pronoun inversion instead)
chunk_compatibility: Formal questions for interviews and professional contexts
primary_function: Forming formal yes/no questions with inversion
essential_vocabulary: venir, terminer, sortir, arriver, faire, avoir, être, aller
why_it_matters: Subject inversion is required for formal French yes/no questions

structure_label: Question words: qui, que, quoi (interrogative pronouns)
core_meaning: Question words for asking about people (qui) and things (que/quoi)
primary_communicative_fn: Asking about people and things with appropriate pronouns
when_to_use: Use qui for people, que for things; quoi for emphasis/ellipsis
pattern: Qui + verbe + subject? / Que + subject + verbe? / Quoi + ellipsis?
key_variations: Qui est-ce qui parle? (Who is speaking?).
Que faites-vous ici? (What are you doing here?).
Quoi de neuf? (What's new? - colloquial).
Qui vois-tu? (Who do you see?).
essential_vocabulary_slots: parler, faire, voir, venir, savoir, dire, connaître
common_learner_mistakes: Confusing qui (who) with que (what) in their contexts
Using « quoi » instead of « qu'est » before vowels: *« quoi est » instead of « qu'est »
Applying wrong word order after question words
chunk_compatibility: Identifying people and things in conversations
primary_function: Distinguishing question pronouns for people vs things
essential_vocabulary: parler, faire, voir, venir, savoir, dire, connaître, penser
why_it_matters: Qui/que/quoi distinctions are fundamental to asking questions

core_meaning: Agreement-based question word meaning 'which' or 'what kind of'
primary_communicative_fn: Asking about specific items with agreement-based selection
when_to_use: Use quel + noun (agrees), not qui/que, to ask which one(s)
key_variations: Quel livre vous faut-il? (Which book do you need?).
Quelle heure est-il? (What time is it?).
Quels amis sont venus? (Which friends came?).
Quelles solutions proposez-vous? (What solutions are you proposing?).
essential_vocabulary_slots: livre, heure, amis, solutions, couleur, jour, mois
common_learner_mistakes: Not agreeing quel with noun: *« quel livres » instead of « quels livres »
Using « quel » as pronoun: *« quel est votre père » (correct: « qui est votre père »)
Confusing quel (adjective) with lequel (pronoun)
chunk_compatibility: Selecting from specific options and categories
primary_function: Asking which from a specific set with agreement
key_forms: Quel, Quelle, Quels, Quelles (always agrees with noun)
essential_vocabulary: livre, heure, amis, solutions, couleur, jour, mois, prix
why_it_matters: Quel must agree with noun—fundamental for selection questions

structure_label: Où, Quand, Comment, Combien, Pourquoi (adverbial questions)
core_meaning: Question adverbs for place, time, manner, quantity, and reason
primary_communicative_fn: Asking for specific information about circumstances
when_to_use: Place interrogative adverb first, then est-ce que or inversion
key_variations: Où habitez-vous? (Where do you live?).
Quand arrive le train? (When does the train arrive?).
Comment vous sentez-vous? (How are you feeling?).
Combien ça coûte? (How much does it cost?) / Pourquoi pas? (Why not?).
essential_vocabulary_slots: où, quand, comment, combien, pourquoi, habiter, arriver, sentir
common_learner_mistakes: Placing question word at end: *« vous habitez où » in formal speech
Confusing comment (how/in what manner) with pourquoi (for what reason)
Not applying inversion with adverbial question words
essential_vocabulary: où, quand, comment, combien, pourquoi, habiter, arriver, sentir
why_it_matters: Adverbial questions require specific word order for formality

structure_label: Est-ce que vs inversion vs intonation (three question methods)
core_meaning: French has three ways to form yes/no questions with varying formality
primary_communicative_fn: Choosing appropriate question formality for context
when_to_use: Select method based on formality: intonation (casual), est-ce que (neutral), inversion (formal)
pattern: Intonation: statement + rising tone | Est-ce que: prefix + statement | Inversion: verb + subject
key_variations: Tu viens? (intonation) / Est-ce que tu viens? (neutral) / Viens-tu? (formal).
Il pleut? / Est-ce qu'il pleut? / Pleut-il?.
Vous voulez? / Est-ce que vous voulez? / Voulez-vous?.
essential_vocabulary_slots: venir, pleuvoir, vouloir, faire, aller, être, avoir
common_learner_mistakes: Mixing registers: *« est-ce que viens-tu »
Assuming all three are equally appropriate in written French
Not using inversion in formal contexts
primary_function: Selecting question formation based on formality level
key_forms: Three options: intonation (casual), est-ce que (neutral), inversion (formal)
essential_vocabulary: venir, pleuvoir, vouloir, faire, aller, être, avoir, venir
why_it_matters: Register selection impacts comprehension and appropriateness

core_meaning: Embedding yes/no questions in larger sentences using si
primary_communicative_fn: Reporting yes/no questions without direct inversion
when_to_use: In reported speech for yes/no questions, use si + normal word order
pattern: Verbe principal + si + sujet + verbe (sans inversion)
key_variations: Je me demande si tu viens (I wonder if you're coming).
Dis-moi s'il a fini (Tell me if he finished).
Je ne sais pas si nous partons (I don't know if we're leaving).
Explique-moi si tu comprends (Explain to me if you understand).
essential_vocabulary_slots: demander, savoir, expliquer, dire, comprendre, venir, finir
common_learner_mistakes: Using inverted order: *« je me demande si viens-tu »
Confusing si (whether) with que (that) in reported speech
Applying direct question word order in indirect speech
chunk_compatibility: Embedded questions in narrative and discussion
primary_function: Reporting yes/no questions with normal subject-verb order
essential_vocabulary: demander, savoir, expliquer, dire, comprendre, venir, finir, partir
why_it_matters: Si converts direct questions to indirect without inversion

core_meaning: Embedding wh-questions in larger sentences, keeping normal word order
when_to_use: In reported speech for wh-questions, maintain normal subject-verb order
key_variations: Je demande ce qu'il fait (I ask what he does).
Dis-moi où tu vas (Tell me where you're going).
Explique pourquoi tu pleures (Explain why you're crying).
Je ne sais pas qui vient (I don't know who's coming).
essential_vocabulary_slots: demander, dire, expliquer, savoir, aller, faire, pleurer
common_learner_mistakes: Using question intonation in statement: *« dis-moi où tu vas? »
Using « ce que » instead of « ce qui » for subject questions
Maintaining direct word order/inversion in reported questions
chunk_compatibility: Reported speech with specific information requests
primary_function: Converting direct wh-questions to indirect without inversion
essential_vocabulary: demander, dire, expliquer, savoir, aller, faire, pleurer, venir
why_it_matters: Indirect wh-questions preserve normal word order without inversion

core_meaning: Compound interrogative pronouns replacing article + noun for selection
primary_communicative_fn: Asking which one from a set using pronoun form
when_to_use: Use lequel (agrees) as pronoun when noun is omitted or distant
key_variations: Lequel préfères-tu? (Which one do you prefer?).
Laquelle de ces robes vous plaît? (Which of these dresses do you like?).
Lesquels sont venus? (Which ones came?).
Auxquelles avez-vous pensé? (Which ones did you think of?).
essential_vocabulary_slots: préférer, plaire, venir, penser, aimer, choisir, vouloir
common_learner_mistakes: Not agreeing in gender/number: *« lequel » for feminine noun
Confusing « lequel » (pronoun) with « quel » (adjective before noun)
Using wrong preposition with lequel: *« de laquelle » vs « à laquelle »
chunk_compatibility: Selecting specific items from multiple options
primary_function: Asking which one(s) with full agreement
key_forms: Lequel, Laquelle, Lesquels, Lesquelles (agrees with antecedent)
essential_vocabulary: préférer, plaire, venir, penser, aimer, choisir, vouloir, sélectionner
why_it_matters: Lequel agreements are complex but essential for selection questions

core_meaning: Compound question form specifically for asking about direct object
primary_communicative_fn: Asking what someone does or wants (direct object focus)
when_to_use: Ask about direct objects: what someone is doing/wanting/buying, etc.
key_variations: Qu'est-ce que tu fais? (What are you doing? - Answer: Je fais mes devoirs).
Qu'est-ce qu'il dit? (What is he saying? - Answer: Il dit la vérité).
Qu'est-ce que vous voulez? (What do you want? - Answer: Je veux partir).
Qu'est-ce qu'elle achète? (What is she buying? - Answer: Elle achète du pain).
essential_vocabulary_slots: faire, dire, vouloir, acheter, manger, boire, lire
common_learner_mistakes: Using for subject questions: *« qu'est-ce que se passe »
Confusing with « qu'est-ce qui » for subject position
Omitting the que before vowels: *« qu'est-ce il » instead of « qu'est-ce qu'il »
chunk_compatibility: Asking about actions, desires, and acquisitions
primary_function: Asking about direct objects in complete sentences
essential_vocabulary: faire, dire, vouloir, acheter, manger, boire, lire, écrire
why_it_matters: Qu'est-ce que targets direct objects—essential for action questions

core_meaning: Compound question form for asking about subject
primary_communicative_fn: Asking what (as subject) is happening or causing something
when_to_use: Ask about subjects: what is happening, causing problems, etc.
pattern: Qu'est-ce qui + verbe (answer will be the subject)?
key_variations: Qu'est-ce qui se passe? (What is happening?).
Qu'est-ce qui cause ce bruit? (What is causing this noise?).
Qu'est-ce qui t'inquiète? (What is worrying you?).
Qu'est-ce qui est tombé? (What fell?).
essential_vocabulary_slots: passer, causer, inquiéter, tomber, arriver, briser, manquer
common_learner_mistakes: Using « que » instead of « qu'est-ce qui »: *« que se passe »
Forgetting « qui » in subject position
Confusing with qu'est-ce que for objects
chunk_compatibility: Asking about events, causes, and emotional impacts
primary_function: Asking about subjects in active voice
essential_vocabulary: passer, causer, inquiéter, tomber, arriver, briser, manquer, intéresser
why_it_matters: Qu'est-ce qui asks about subjects—essential for event questions

structure_label: Interrogation totale vs partielle (total vs partial questions)
core_meaning: Total questions ask yes/no; partial questions ask for specific information
primary_communicative_fn: Choosing appropriate question type for expected answer
when_to_use: Total: expect yes/no | Partial: expect specific information
pattern: Total: est-ce que/inversion/intonation | Partial: interrogative + statement
key_variations: Tu viens? (total → yes/no answer).
Quand est-ce que tu viens? (partial → time answer).
Viendras-tu? (total formal → yes/no).
Où allez-vous ce soir? (partial → place answer).
essential_vocabulary_slots: venir, aller, faire, être, avoir, savoir, pouvoir
common_learner_mistakes: Using interrogative words for yes/no questions: *« où tu viens? »
Not recognizing intonation questions as valid informal option
Confusing question type with expected answer format
chunk_compatibility: Appropriate question types for various information needs
primary_function: Distinguishing total from partial question purposes
key_forms: Total: 3 methods | Partial: interrogative + normal order
essential_vocabulary: venir, aller, faire, être, avoir, savoir, pouvoir, être
why_it_matters: Understanding question types improves comprehension accuracy

core_meaning: Tag question added to statement expecting confirmation
primary_communicative_fn: Seeking agreement or confirmation on stated facts
when_to_use: Add n'est-ce pas to statements to request confirmation
pattern: Phrase affirmative + , n'est-ce pas? / Phrase négative + , non?
key_variations: Il fait beau, n'est-ce pas? (It's nice weather, isn't it?).
Vous venez, n'est-ce pas? (You're coming, aren't you?).
Ce n'est pas vrai, non? (That's not true, is it?).
Vous l'avez fait, n'est-ce pas? (You did it, didn't you?).
essential_vocabulary_slots: faire, venir, être, avoir, devoir, pouvoir, savoir
common_learner_mistakes: Using « n'est-ce pas » after negative: *« il ne vient pas, n'est-ce pas? »
Confusing tag questions with actual answer-seeking questions
Using « n'est-ce pas » with all statements regardless of polarity
essential_vocabulary: faire, venir, être, avoir, devoir, pouvoir, savoir, aller
why_it_matters: Tag questions are social politeness devices in French conversation

core_meaning: Informal tags and contradictions in spoken French
primary_communicative_fn: Seeking confirmation informally and contradicting negatives
when_to_use: Use hein casually; use si only to contradict negative statements
key_variations: C'est bien, hein? (That's good, huh?).
Il est là, hein? (He's here, right?).
— Je ne crois pas. — Si! (I disagree!).
— Tu ne veux pas. — Mais si, je veux! (Yes, I do want to!).
essential_vocabulary_slots: hein, si, croire, vouloir, être, faire, venir
common_learner_mistakes: Using « si » to agree with yes-questions: *« — Tu viens? — Si! »
Overusing « hein » in formal or written French
Forgetting « mais » before « si » in emphatic contradictions
chunk_compatibility: Casual spoken discourse with emphatic responses
essential_vocabulary: hein, si, croire, vouloir, être, faire, venir, penser
why_it_matters: Hein and si are frequent in colloquial French—register-specific usage

core_meaning: Asking for reason with est-ce que pattern
when_to_use: Use pourquoi + est-ce que for neutral why questions
key_variations: Pourquoi est-ce que tu pleures? (Why are you crying?).
Pourquoi est-ce qu'elle est partie? (Why did she leave?).
Pourquoi est-ce que vous faites cela? (Why are you doing that?).
Pourquoi est-ce qu'ils protestent? (Why are they protesting?).
essential_vocabulary_slots: pleurer, partir, faire, protester, croire, penser, venir
common_learner_mistakes: Confusing pourquoi (why/reason) with comment (how/manner)
Using « pourquoi pas » incorrectly for expressing alternatives
Omitting est-ce que when inversion would be clearer
primary_function: Asking for reasons and cause-effect relationships
key_forms: Pourquoi est-ce que + statement word order
essential_vocabulary: pleurer, partir, faire, protester, croire, penser, venir, avoir
why_it_matters: Why questions are fundamental to conversation and understanding
